cases.title: Small Town, Big Bus Crash
cases.presentation: A bus crash outside a small rural town produces 22 injured passengers. The local critical-access hospital has one physician on duty, four nurses, and two ambulances. The nearest trauma center is 90 minutes away.
cases.question: How should this event be classified and managed?
options: A: A routine emergency — treat patients in order of arrival.
B: A disaster/MCI for this community — needs exceed local resources, so activate the emergency plan, use formal triage, and request mutual aid.
C: Not a disaster because there are fewer than 100 casualties.
D: Wait for a federal declaration before changing procedures.
cases.explanation: Twenty-two casualties overwhelm one physician, four nurses, and two ambulances — needs exceed resources, which is the definition of a disaster regardless of absolute numbers. The correct posture is plan activation, triage for the greatest good, and early mutual-aid requests.
cases.clinicalPearl: Disaster is a local arithmetic problem: the same crash near a level-1 trauma center might be just a busy afternoon.

cases.title: The Plan on the Shelf
cases.presentation: During accreditation review, a hospital presents a comprehensive 200-page disaster plan. Interviews reveal no staff member has participated in a drill for three years, and the plan's phone tree lists departed employees.
cases.question: What is the most important corrective action?
options: A: Add more chapters to the plan.
B: Exercise the plan regularly, update contacts, and train staff — planning and practice are inseparable.
C: Nothing — a written plan satisfies preparedness.
D: Replace the plan with verbal instructions.
cases.explanation: The PRE-DISASTER Paradigm pairs planning with practice. An unexercised, outdated plan fails under stress; drills reveal gaps (like the dead phone tree) and build the role familiarity a real response requires.
cases.clinicalPearl: A disaster plan is only as good as its last exercise.

cases.title: The Responder Who Couldn't Respond
cases.presentation: After an earthquake, a hospital activates its surge plan, but a third of the staff do not report: schools are closed, roads are damaged, and many have no family emergency arrangements. Care capacity collapses despite an intact building.
cases.question: What preparedness lesson does this illustrate?
options: A: Surge capacity is only about beds and supplies.
B: Staff personal and family preparedness is a core component of facility surge capacity.
C: Staff who stay home should be dismissed.
D: The surge plan was unnecessary.
cases.explanation: Surge capacity = space, supplies, systems, AND staff. Employees without family communication plans, dependent-care arrangements, and go-kits often cannot report, so facility preparedness programs must include personal preparedness.
cases.clinicalPearl: Beds don't treat patients — people do. Personal preparedness is institutional capacity.
